coredata.dc:title: Effect of adding carbon quantum dots to a NMP solution of cellulose acetate on the formation mechanism of ensuing membrane
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Hong-Li
Ang, Micah Belle Marie Yap
Tsai, Hui-An
Lee, Kueir-Rarn
Lai, Juin-Yih
coredata.dc:description:
Background Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can improve membrane separation efficiency and significantly contribute to solving global problems. Investigating the influence of adding CQDs on phase separation of cellulose acetate (CA)/ N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solution would provide more understanding of membrane formation.
Methods CA+CQDs membrane was fabricated through non-solvent induced phase separation, using water as coagulant. Kinetic and thermodynamics behavior of polymer solution affected the phase separation mechanism. The thermodynamic behavior was observed in cloud-point curve from the ternary phase diagram of CA/NMP/water, whereas the kinetic was studied by correlating the viscosity of the dope with light transmittance experiment and optical microscope observation. Fourier transform infrared microscope measured the time of phase separation at different depths of the casting plate.
Significant findings Adding CQDs can affect both the kinetics and thermodynamic behavior CA/NMP solution. At the top of the casting plate, phase separation behavior was affected mainly by the formation of pseudo water layer, causing fast demixing rate with more porous structure. In the inner part, phase separation was facilitated by kinetic of the dope. The 1,000 ppm CQDs added CA membrane delivered the highest pure water flux of 731.90 ± 68.27 L·m−2·h−1 (1 bar).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Cellulose acetate
Membrane formation
Pseudo water layer
Fourier transform infrared microscope
Phase inversion

Study on phase inversion mechanism of the polymer solution
Pure water flux test and determination of molecular weight cutoff

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Crossflow filtration set-up for determination of pure water flux.
TEM image (A), particle size distribution (B), FTIR spectrum (C), and XPS C1 narrow scan of CQDs.
Effect of CQDs concentration on the surface morphology of CA membrane. CQDs concentration: (A, A’) 0 ppm, (B, B’) 1000 ppm, (C, C’) 2000 ppm, (D, D’) 3000 ppm.

Effect of CQDs concentration on the cross-sectional morphology of CA membranes. CQDs concentration: (A) (B) (C) 0 ppm, (D) (E) (F) 1000 ppm, (G) (H) (I) 2000 ppm, and (J) (K) (L) 3000 ppm. Skin layer: (A) (D) (G) (J); The wall of macrovoids: (C) (F) (I) (L).
Penetration distance of non-solvent vs time (A) and time1/2 (B).
Results from light transmission experiment for CA/NMP and CA/NMP+CQDs solution. Non-solvent: water.

Weight fraction of (●) CA, (▲) NMP and (▓) Water at different depth beneath the surface of polymer solution; (A)(B) 0 μm, (C)(D) 40 μm, (E)(F) 120 μm, (G)(H) 200 μm FTIR-microscopy. CA/NMP/H2O system: (A)(C)(E)(G); CA/NMP+CQDs/H2O system: (B)(D)(F)(H).

Weight fraction of CA, NMP, and H2O at different depth beneath the surface of polymer solution. (A) CA/NMP/Water and (B) CA/NMP+CQDs/Water for 24 s, and (C) CA/NMP/Water and (D) CA/NMP+CQDs/Water for 72 s.

The time dependence composition paths of polymer solution at different position after contacting with water. (A)(B) 0 μm, (C)(D) 40 μm, (E)(F) 120 μm, (G)(H) 200 μm depth beneath the surface of CA dope. Cloud-point curve and composition path of (▓,▲) CA/NMP/H2O, (●,▲) CA/NMP+CQDs/H2O at 25 °C.
(A) Pure water flux and (B) MWCO of CA and CA+1000 ppm CQDs membrane.
Composition and viscosity of the polymer solution.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a concentration of CQDs was added based on the polymer concentration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of CQDs concentration on surface porosity of CA membrane.
Diffusivity of non-solvent used to undergo phase separation.

head.ce:title: Effect of adding carbon quantum dots to a NMP solution of cellulose acetate on the formation mechanism of ensuing membrane

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: R&D Center for Membrane Technology and Department of Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can improve membrane separation efficiency and significantly contribute to solving global problems. Investigating the influence of adding CQDs on phase separation of cellulose acetate (CA)/ N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solution would provide more understanding of membrane formation.

CA+CQDs membrane was fabricated through non-solvent induced phase separation, using water as coagulant. Kinetic and thermodynamics behavior of polymer solution affected the phase separation mechanism. The thermodynamic behavior was observed in cloud-point curve from the ternary phase diagram of CA/NMP/water, whereas the kinetic was studied by correlating the viscosity of the dope with light transmittance experiment and optical microscope observation. Fourier transform infrared microscope measured the time of phase separation at different depths of the casting plate.

Adding CQDs can affect both the kinetics and thermodynamic behavior CA/NMP solution. At the top of the casting plate, phase separation behavior was affected mainly by the formation of pseudo water layer, causing fast demixing rate with more porous structure. In the inner part, phase separation was facilitated by kinetic of the dope. The 1,000 ppm CQDs added CA membrane delivered the highest pure water flux of 731.90 ± 68.27 L·m−2·h−1 (1 bar).

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane separation technology received substantial attention because of its higher separation efficiency, lower energy consumption, and more eco-friendly than that of traditional separation processes. Phase inversion process is extensively used to prepare membranes, such as non-solvent induced phase separation; thermal-induced phase separation; vapor induced phase separation; and evaporation induced phase separation [1]. In the non-solvent induced phase separation process, after the polymer solution is cast with a predetermined thickness, it is immediately immersed into the non-solvent coagulant bath to form a membrane (wet phase inversion process). Alternatively, after casting the polymer solution it could be placed in a dry environment for a period of time, followed by immersing it in the coagulation bath to form a membrane (dry/wet phase inversion process). In general, membranes prepared through the non-solvent induced phase separation process can be used for microfiltration and ultrafiltration. This can be also used as a sub-layer in preparing composite membranes on some occasion.

Membrane performances can be affected by the morphology of the membrane, such as pore type, pore size, thickness, and porosity [2–4]. The formation of different membrane morphology was mainly facilitated by the kinetics behavior [5] as well as thermodynamics behavior [6] of the phase inversion process. In terms of kinetics perspective, the solvent outflow and non-solvent inflow process used in the membrane formation model describe the behavior of solvent and non-solvent during the membrane formation process [7]. Furthermore, the model can be used to predict the phase separation rate of the polymer solution, consequently predicting the membrane morphology. A porous structure might form easily when a faster rate of phase separation occurred [8].

In thermodynamics perspective, when the casting polymer solution is placed in a coagulation bath containing a non-solvent (usually the non-solvent is water), a ternary phase diagram composed of polymer, solvent and non-solvent can be established to indicate the thermodynamics behavior of the membrane formation [9]. Any point within the ternary phase diagram shows a mixture of the three components (polymer, solvent, and non-solvent). Binodal curve and spinodal curve can be found in the ternary phase diagram. Generally, a polymer solution is prepared to fabricate a membrane; however, it is also possible to add some non-solvents or additives if all the components in the solution are still miscible. Either adding a certain amount of non-solvent or immersing the casting solution to a non-solvent bath, the polymer solution becomes thermodynamically unstable and phase separation occurs when reaching the binodal curve [7]. When phase separation occurs instantaneously, membranes with a relatively porous morphology can be obtained. On the other hand, when demixing occurs after a period of time, relatively dense morphology membrane can be prepared.

Cellulose acetate (CA), a hydrophilic material, is a biodegradable and an eco-friendly polymer. Compared with other polymer, CA is relatively cheap and can be easily dissolved in most solvents. Its membrane morphology and performance can also be influenced by the choice of solvent/non-solvent system [10,11], polymer composition [12,13], composition [14] and type of coagulation bath and temperature [14], and the addition of organic/inorganic particles [15,16]. The incorporation of additive into the membrane is a beneficial modification method for improving the performance of the membrane. Three types of additives can be used: hydrophilic, amphiphilic, and inorganic materials. These can be directly added to the polymer solution. The most commonly used hydrophilic additives are polyethylene glycol [17–19], polyvinylpyrrolidone [6,19–22], alcohols [23] and water [24]. Surfactants including Span, Tween and Pluronic F-127 have been used as amphiphilic additives [6,25–30]. The inorganic materials incorporated into the membrane include SiO2 [31], silver [32], Al2O3 [33], LiCl, and carbon-based materials [34–47].

The addition of additive into the casting solution can affect the kinetic and/or thermodynamic behavior of the membrane formation to control the membrane properties. In the 1990s and 2000s, the formation mechanism of organic additives, including the hydrophilic organic polymer and amphiphilic polymer, was first investigated [28–30,48,49]. Usually, introducing hydrophilic polymer aims to increase the affinity between polymer solution and water coagulation bath. The purpose of the introduction of an amphiphilic polymer is to increase the affinity between polymer and additive. Amphiphilic polymer can easily migrate to the top surface of the membrane but hard to leach out by a coagulant because of its high interaction with the polymer or additive. Subsequently, hydrophilic material become popular because it can improve both membrane hydrophilicity and properties. Then the inorganic additive was investigated for membrane formation during 2010s to recently [50–52]. However, a same material or type of additives could give different purpose and have different role during membrane formation.

Carbon-based materials are one of the most attractive research materials recently. Graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotube (CNT) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were two, one and zero dimensional carbon-based material, respectively [34]. The carbon-based material can be introduced into polyamide layer and applied in nanofiltration to improve the membrane performance [35,44]. The formation of GO [36,37,43,53–55] and CNT [38,39,45,56] acting as the additive has been investigated widely. Although CQDs have many advantages, such as having a high hydrophilicity, easily adjustable size, and functional groups, most research on CQDs still focused on introducing it into polyamide layer [40,41,46] and the effect of CQDs on membrane separation efficiency for gas separation and filtration [57–61], whereas only a few studies tackle on membrane formation of polymer, where CQDs act as the additive [42,47,61]. There is a lack of systematic discussion in the literature on the effect of adding CQDs in the membrane formation of CA. The thermodynamic and kinetic behavior of CA polymer solution with CQDs have not been investigated extensively. Thus, in this work, CQDs was used as additives to prepare CA membrane through wet phase inversion process, and the effects of adding CQDs on the thermodynamics and kinetics behavior of CA membrane formation were systematically studied.

CA powder (CA-394-60S) was procured from Eastman Chemical Company, Palo Alto, CA, USA. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, reagent grade) was the solvent for CA (Tedia Company Inc., Fairfield, OH, USA). Citric acid (anhydrous) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Showa Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Polyester nonwoven was bought from Ahlstrom (Helsinki, Finland). Polyethylene glycol (PEG) 35k, PEG 100k and PEG 300k was provided by Sigma-Aldrich (Saint Louis, MO, USA). PEG 600k was supplied by Acros Organics (Fair Lawn, NJ, USA).

CQDs were synthesized through a bottom-up approach—direct pyrolysis of citric acid [62]. The oven was preheated to 210 °C and was maintained at a steady temperature for 30 min. Two grams of citric acid was loaded in a Teflon-lined autoclave reactor. Afterwards, it was placed inside the oven for 3 h. The solid citric acid was turned into an orange liquid with paste-like texture, signifying the successful formation of CQDs. Afterward, the synthesized CQDs were neutralized to pH 7 using 10 mg/mL NaOH and then deionized water was added until the volume reach 100 mL. The CQDs solution was purified using a dialysis tube (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 6 h, where water was changed every hour. Dried CQDs powder was obtained through freeze-drying of the purified CQDs solution.

Table 1 list the composition and viscosity of the polymer solutions. CQDs (0–3000 ppm) was dispersed in NMP using homogenizer at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. CQDs/NMP solution was placed in the oil bath at 90 °C. Afterwards, the CA powder was poured into the CQDs/NMP solution. The concentration of the CA in the solution was fixed at 13 wt%. After the complete dissolution of the CA, the CA/NMP+CQDs solution was degassed overnight. The CA/NMP and CA/NMP+CQDs solution was cast on a nonwoven polyester (casting knife gap = 300 μm) and immediately immersed in a water coagulation bath at 25 °C. Both CA and CA+CQDs membranes were washed several times with distilled water to ensure the removal of excess NMP. All membranes were stored in distilled water prior characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CQDs and membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The elemental composition of CQDs was determined using x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Model: Thermo Scientific, K-Alpha). The CQDs solution was dropped on the silicon wafer and dried at 50 °C for 24 h in vacuum oven.

Morphology and size of CQDs morphology were defined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Model: JEOL JEM-2100, Tokyo, Japan). The CQDs solution was dropped on copper mesh and dried at 50 °C for 24 h in vacuum oven.

Surface and cross-sectional morphology of the membranes were observed using field-emission scanning electron microscope (Model S-4800, Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan). Samples were freeze-dried for 24 h The dried membrane was placed on SEM stage using carbon tape. For cross-sectional morphology, samples were freeze-fractured under liquid nitrogen. The pore size and surface porosity of the membrane were measured using ImageJ software. For one condition of the membrane, 4 samples were measured.

The surface roughness of the membrane was quantified using atomic force microscope (Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA). Samples were first freeze-dried, then dried membrane was taped on an iron plate. Root means square (Rq) was determined four times for each membrane condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on phase inversion mechanism of the polymer solution
ce:section.ce:para: The viscosity of the polymer solution was measured by a rheometer (Fig. S1, Brookfield R/S-cps, MA, USA) at 25 °C using C50-1 spindle. The cloud-point measurement of the polymer solution was examined through titration method at 25 °C [63]. Demixing rate of the polymer solution was studied through light transmission experiment (Fig. S2), where non-solvent was water at 25 °C and surrounding was covered with a black box. The polymer solution was cast on the glass plate and immediately immersed in the water coagulation bath. During phase inversion process, the light transmittance was recorded using Lutron software/RS232 (Model: Lutron LX-1102 light meter).

The penetration of the water into the polymer solution was recorded using an optical microscope [64]. The polymer solution was placed between two glass slides. A drop of distilled water containing rhodamine B was introduced using a syringe to the side of the glass (Fig. S3). Afterwards, it diffused into the polymer solution. The diffusion and travelled distance of the water were recorded. Diffusivity coefficient (D) of the water was calculated using Fick's law (Eq. 1):
(1)
d =
4 D t
where d was the penetration distance (cm) of water into the polymer solution at time t (sec).

The composition of the polymer solution at different area during phase inversion process was analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) microscope (Perkin Elmer, Model LE186-0078). First, the polymer solution was placed in the liquid cell composed of two CaF2 plates sandwiched by a 15 μm thick Teflon spacer. The size of the focal spot was 20 μm × 300 μm (width x length) to limit the FTIR transmission area to collect the localized spectra. Then, composition of the polymer solution was recorded for 5 min (Fig. S4) [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pure water flux test and determination of molecular weight cutoff
ce:section.ce:para:
Pure water flux (PWF) was measured using a laboratory made crossflow setup (Fig. 1
.). The membrane was placed inside the cell with an effective area of 12.57 cm2 (A) and pre-pressurized at 1.5 bar for 1 h. Afterwards, the PWF was calculated at 1 bar using the following equation (Eq. 2):
(2)
PWF =
V
A × t
where V was the volume collected in permeate (L) at time t.

For determination of molecular weight cutoff (MWCO), similar crossflow filtration setup and procedure from testing the PWF was used and followed, respectively. After the steady state, the feed was changed into 1000 ppm PEG with specific molecular weight. Then, the permeate was collected for a certain time. The concentration of feed (Cf ) and permeate (Cp ) was analyzed using total organic carbon (Model: vario TOC select, Elementar). The rejection of PEG (%R) was calculated as follows (Eq. 3):
(3)
% R =
C f
−
C p
C f
×
100

The membrane pore size could calculate by the following equation (Eq. 4) [66]:
(4)
y =
220
x 3
where y was the MWCO, and x was the pore size of the membrane.

Fig. 2 (A) and (B) shows the TEM image and size analysis of the prepared CQDs. The mean size of prepared CQDs was about 7.19 ± 3.63 nm, which was similar to the reported literature [67]. FTIR spectrum confirmed the chemical functional groups on CQDs (Fig. 2(C)). Peaks at 1047, 1080, 1257, 1388, 1567, 1709 and 3300 cm−1 were corresponded to vibrations of C-O, OH, C-O-C, C-OH, COO−, C=O and stretching of OH, respectively [68]. These functional groups in CQDs indicate its hydrophilic property. The elemental analysis using XPS also confirmed that the CQDs had C, O, Na element (Fig. S5, and Table S1). COOH and COH bonding in CQDs was verified in C1s spectra (Fig. 2(D)). The CC, CO and C=O bonds are located at 284.1 eV, 285.6 eV and 287.6 eV, respectively. The peak at 1070 eV (Fig. S5) was corresponding to Na, which was formed during the titration of CQDs using NaOH solution, where some of the COOH group was converted to COONa.

CA and CA+CQDs membrane had porous and macrovoids structure (Fig. 4 ). Increasing the CQDs concentration from 0 to 3000 ppm, the skin layer (Fig. 4(A) (D) (G) (J)) became thinner (from 133 ± 11.1 to 81 ± 3.8 nm) while the bicontinuous pores in the wall of macrovoids became larger (Fig. 4(C) (F) (I) (L)). The pseudo water layer was easily formed because of the hydrophilicity of CQDs. Subsequently, the non-solvent inflow increased with concentration of CQDs content. Therefore, the modified membrane had a thinner and porous membrane structure of the skin layer [73].

The kinetic behavior of the membrane formation has been observed using an optical microscope (Fig. S7). The exchange rate of the solvent and non-solvent was observed from 0 to 90 s. When the non-solvent comes in contact with the polymer solution, the phase separation started immediately. The traveling distance of non-solvent into the polymer solution increased with time. Fig. 5 illustrates the dependence of travelled length of non-solvent with time that was captured by the image in Fig. S7. The non-solvent penetration distance (D) vs square root of time (t1/2) was shown in Fig. 5(B). The penetration distance increases with square root of t linearly.

Table 3 summarizes the diffusivity of non-solvent (water) into the CA and CA/NMP+CQDs solution. There was a decrease in diffusivity of non-solvent with increasing concentration of CQDs in CA solution. When the non-solvent was in contact with the polymer solution within 30 s, the penetration distance of non-solvent into the CA/NMP+1000 ppm CQDs system was longer than that of the CA/NMP system. For 15 to 30 s, the penetration distances of non-solvent into the CA/NMP solution and the CA/NMP+1000 ppm CQDs solution were 0.00847 to 0.01403 cm and 0.00975 to 0.01451 cm, respectively. Because of the increased in water concentration on the interface from the pseudo water layer, it results in faster inflow of non-solvent to the polymer solution. However, when the non-solvent was in contact for over 30 s, the penetration distance of the CA/NMP system was longer than that of the CA/NMP+1000 ppm CQDs system. The penetration distance of non-solvent at 45 to 90 s for was as follow: CA/NMP solution = 0.01846 to 0.02667 cm; and CA/NMP+1000 ppm CQDs solution = 0.01815 to 0.02617 cm. Adding CQDs increased the viscosity of the polymer solution (Table 1), thus, diffusion of non-solvent was slow, leading to a reduction of diffusivity of non-solvent. Furthermore, higher viscosity of the polymer solution makes the subsequent non-solvent less likely to enter inside the polymer solution.

Light transmission experiment could tell the demixing rate or if the membrane was dense or porous (Fig. 6 ). All polymer solutions had an instantaneous demixing, resulting in the formation of macrovoids (Fig. 4). Thus, with or without CQDs in CA/NMP solution, macrovoids can form in the CA membranes. The membranes with CQDs had a higher light transmission than that of pristine CA membrane. There could be two possible reasons: (1) addition of CQDs slowed down the overall demixing rate of the polymer solution because of the increased in solution viscosity; or (2) membranes containing CQDs had a more asymmetrical and porous structure than that of pristine CA membrane. Either of this two could explain the result of the light transmission experiment. Therefore, in the following section, FTIR microscope would confirm whether the demixing rate increased or not, because it could give more accurate result than the light transmission experiment.

How does the addition of CQDs affect the thermodynamic behavior of CA membrane formation? Fig. 7 depicts the cloud-point curve of CA/NMP/H2O and CA/NMP+1000 ppm CQDs/H2O system. There is an increase in demixing gap as CQDs was added into the polymer solution. Because CQDs acts as the additive, the solubility of the polymer solution decreased and the stability of thermodynamic broke down after CQDs was added; Thus, leading to shift of CA/NMP+1000 ppm CQDs/H2O cloud point curve close to CA/NMP axis. These results can depict that adding CQDs in the CA/NMP solution can also affect the thermodynamic behavior of membrane formation of CA.

CA/NMP or CA/NMP+1,000 ppm CQDs solution was placed into a liquid cell composed of two CaF2 plates and a spacer. Then, water was injected between the plates by a syringe, where the composition of the polymer solution was identified using FTIR microscope. By contacting the water with the polymer solution in the liquid cell of FTIR microscope, the time dependence of CA, NMP and water weight fraction was recorded at different depths of 0, 40, 120 and 200 μm beneath the surface of the CA solution (Fig. 8 ). Regardless of the position in the CA solution, after the CA solution was in contact with the water, the water and NMP fractions varies with the contact time in the system with or without adding CQDs. With the increased in contact time, the ratio of water with the NMP increased. Because when the water was in contact with the polymer solution, the solvent/non-solvent exchange occurred immediately. Thus, the water diffused into the polymer solution, whereas the NMP flows out from the polymer solution, resulting in an increase of amount of water and decreased in amount of NMP.

Fig. 9 is the bar graph for the weight fraction as a function of the depth beneath the surface of polymer solution for 24 and 72 s. At any depth beneath the surface of polymer solution, the weight fraction of water in CA/NMP+CQDs system is higher than that of CA/NMP system. Because CQDs is a hydrophilic additive, when a hydrophilic additive was added to the polymer solution, the hydrophilicity of the polymer solution was enhanced, thus water can flow into the polymer solution easily. Furthermore, the concentration of water in the interface of non-solvent and CA/NMP+CQDs solution was higher than that of CA/NMP solution, because of the hydrophilic CQDs attracts more water molecules, leading to creation of pseudo water layer on the top part of the polymer solution. For CA/NMP+CQDs solution, the concentration of water at the top layer increased above 30 wt% (Fig. 8(B), (D)) with water driving force. This phenomenon leads to an increase in the fraction of water at the top layer; consequently, creating more porous structure on the membrane surface (Fig. 2 and Table 2).

On the top layer of the polymer solution, the driving force of water upsurge with an increasing concentration of water in the interface of water-polymer solution, leading to faster diffusion of water into the CA/NMP+CQDs solution. Therefore, the membrane formation was dominated by the creation of pseudo water layer when CQDs were added to the CA/NMP solution. However, in the inner part of the phase separation process, because of the higher viscosity of CA/NMP+CQDs solution than that of CA/NMP solution, the diffusion distance of non-solvent for CA/NMP+CQDs was shorter. Thus, the diffusion of non-solvent into the inner part of the polymer solution was dominated by the kinetic behavior.

Fig. 10 relates the composition path at different depth beneath the surface, and the time requires for the non-solvent reach the cloud-point curve. The non-solvent could reach the cloud-point faster in CA/NMP+CQDs solution than that of CA/NMP solution, at any depth beneath the surface of polymer solution. For both CA/NMP and CA/NMP+CQDs solution, the time required for the composition path to reach the cloud-point curve was increasing with the depth beneath the surface of the polymer solution.

At 0 µm, the composition path for CA/NMP solution passed through the cloud-point curve (Fig. 10(A)) for 6.4 s, whereas the composition path for CA/NMP+CQDs solution pass through the cloud-point curve for before 5.3 s. At the 200 µm, the composition path for CA/NMP and CA/NMP+CQDs solution required 106.7 and 61.2 s to reach the cloud-point, respectively. Because adding CQDs into the CA solution shifted the cloud-point curve to the left (Fig. 7), leading to a large phase separation region. Therefore, during membrane formation, the polymer solution with CQDs could reach the cloud-point curve faster than polymer solution without CQDs.

The change in membrane morphology and surface property could affect the membrane efficiency. Fig. 11 (A) plots the PWF as a function of CQDs concentration. The PWF had a similar trend with surface porosity (Table 2). Increasing the CQDs concentration from 0 to 1000 ppm, the PWF enhanced from 395.78 ± 33.24 to 731.90 ± 68.27 L·m−2·h−1. CA+1000 CQDs membrane had the most porous surface, and more hydrophilic than that of CA membrane (Fig. S8), resulting in higher PWF. However, when the concentration of CQDs exceeds over 1000 ppm, the PWF was declined to 560.41 ± 96.33 L·m−2·h−1 (CA+3000 CQDs). At higher surface porosity, the mass transfer resistance was reduced, making the water to pass through easily. Therefore, the suitable concentration to add CQDs into 13 wt% CA/NMP solution was 1000 ppm.

MWCO was the rejection at 90% of the solutes. Fig. 11(B) illustrates the MWCO of the CA and modified membrane. CA had a MWCO of 428,730.6 Da, whereas CA+1,000 ppm CQDs had 576,003.2 Da. After adding CQDs in the CA solution, the pore size also increased, thus the PWF also increased. This membrane was a promising for ultrafiltration processes. Using Eq. (3), pore size of the membrane was calculated. The pore size of pristine CA membrane was 12.49 nm, whereas the pore size of CA+1,000 ppm CQDs was 13.78 nm.

Adding hydrophilic CQDs (7.19 ± 3.63 nm) on the CA/NMP solution affecting both the kinetic and thermodynamic behavior during membrane formation. The thickness of the skin layer become thinner with increasing CQDs concentration. CQDs in the polymer solution could create a pseudo water layer on the top surface of the polymer solution, leading to more porous surface. FTIR microscope can observed both kinetic and thermodynamic behavior of the polymer solution. According to FTIR microscope experiment, after adding the CQDs, the phase separation behavior on the top layer of the membrane was dominated by thermodynamic property, whereas at kinetic property led the phase separation behavior on the inner part of the membrane. At the optimal concentration of CQDs (1,000 ppm), the modified membrane had the highest surface porosity, roughness, and PWF (731.90 ± 68.27 L·m−2·h−1). Therefore, the modified membrane could be promising for different membrane application.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 106-2221-E-033–062-MY3, MOST 108-2218-E-033-007-MY3, MOST 109-2811-E-033-501).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2022.104250.
